Explain the role of the intrinsic matrix $K$ with its complete mathematical form. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A camera has focal length $f = 50$ mm, pixel size $5 \ \mu m$ ($5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm), and principal point at the image center of a $640 \times 480$ sensor. A 3D point in camera coordinates is $(X, Y, Z) = (0.1, 0.2, 5.0)$ meters. Compute the pixel coordinates $(u, v)$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Perspective Projection:** Consider a 3D point $P$ with camera coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ and its image projection at $(x, y)$ on the image plane at distance $f$ from the pinhole. By similar triangles: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{x}{f} &= \frac{X}{Z} \\ \frac{y}{f} &= \frac{Y}{Z} \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging: $$ \begin{aligned} x &= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \\ y &= f \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to pixel coordinates with pixel densities $m_x$ and $m_y$: $$ \begin{aligned} u &= f \cdot m_x \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x = \alpha \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \\ v &= f \cdot m_y \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y = \beta \cdot \frac{Y}{Z} + c_y \end{aligned} $$ The intrinsic matrix $K$ encapsulates all internal camera properties: $$ \begin{aligned} K &= \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ where $\alpha = f \cdot m_x$ and $\beta = f \cdot m_y$ are focal lengths in pixel units, $c_x, c_y$ is the principal point, and $\gamma$ is the skew coefficient. In homogeneous coordinates, the complete projection is: $$ \begin{aligned} s \cdot \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= K \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $f = 50$ mm, pixel size = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ mm, image center $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$, point $P = (0.1, 0.2, 5.0)$ m. Step 1: Compute pixel scaling factors (note: 1 m = 1000 mm): $$ \begin{aligned} m_x = m_y &= \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \text{ pixels/mm} \\ \alpha = f \cdot m_x &= 50 \times 200 = 10000 \text{ pixels} \\ \beta = f \cdot m_y &= 50 \times 200 = 10000 \text{ pixels} \end{aligned} $$ Step 2: Convert world coordinates to mm: $X = 100$ mm, $Y = 200$ mm, $Z = 5000$ mm. Step 3: Apply projection equations: $$ \begin{aligned} u &= 10000 \cdot \frac{100}{5000} + 320 = 10000 \cdot 0.02 + 320 = 200 + 320 = 520 \text{ pixels} \\ v &= 10000 \cdot \frac{200}{5000} + 240 = 10000 \cdot 0.04 + 240 = 400 + 240 = 640 \text{ pixels} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $(u, v) = (520, 640)$ pixels. --- **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the complete pinhole camera model with world-to-camera transformation. Derive the camera projection matrix $P = K[R \vert t]$ and describe what each component represents. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Given a camera with intrinsic matrix $K = \begin{bmatrix} 1000 & 0 & 320 \\ 0 & 1000 & 240 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and extrinsic parameters $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, $t = (1, 2, 3)^T$, find the pixel coordinates of the 3D world point $P_w = (4, 5, 6)^T$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Complete Pinhole Camera Model:** The complete pinhole camera model transforms a 3D world point $P_w = (X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1)^T$ to 2D pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ through two stages. **Stage 1 — Extrinsic Transformation (World to Camera):** $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Here, $R$ is a $3 \times 3$ rotation matrix (orthogonal, $R^T R = I$) representing the camera's orientation, and $t$ is a $3 \times 1$ translation vector representing the camera's position in world coordinates. **Stage 2 — Intrinsic Transformation (Camera to Pixel):** $$ \begin{aligned} s \cdot \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= K \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_c \\ Y_c \\ Z_c \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Combined Camera Projection Matrix:** $$ \begin{aligned} P &= K \cdot [R \vert t] \end{aligned} $$ This is a $3 \times 4$ matrix that directly maps homogeneous world coordinates to homogeneous pixel coordinates. The complete equation is: $$ \begin{aligned} s \cdot \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} &= K \cdot [R \vert t] \cdot \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Component Summary:** - $K$ (Intrinsic): Internal camera properties (focal length, principal point, skew). - $R$ (Extrinsic): Camera orientation in the world. - $t$ (Extrinsic): Camera position in the world. **(b) Numerical Solution:** Step 1: Form the extrinsic matrix $[R \vert t]$: $$ \begin{aligned} [R \vert t] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ Step 2: Compute camera coordinates $P_c = R \cdot P_w + t$: $$ \begin{aligned} X_c &= 0 \cdot 4 + (-1) \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 6 + 1 = -5 + 1 = -4 \\ Y_c &= 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 5 + 0 \cdot 6 + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \\ Z_c &= 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 5 + 1 \cdot 6 + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 \end{aligned} $$ Step 3: Apply intrinsic matrix $K$ to camera coordinates: $$ \begin{aligned} s \cdot u &= 1000 \cdot (-4) + 0 \cdot 6 + 320 \cdot 9 = -4000 + 2880 = -1120 \\ s \cdot v &= 0 \cdot (-4) + 1000 \cdot 6 + 240 \cdot 9 = 6000 + 2160 = 8160 \\ s &= 0 \cdot (-4) + 0 \cdot 6 + 1 \cdot 9 = 9 \end{aligned} $$ Step 4: Normalize by $s = 9$: $$ \begin{aligned} u &= -1120 / 9 \approx -124.44 \text{ pixels} \\ v &= 8160 / 9 \approx 906.67 \text{ pixels} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $(u, v) \approx (-124.44, 906.67)$ pixels. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:** > > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert between meters and millimeters. Always check units before computing $f \cdot X/Z$. > > 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** The rotation matrix $R$ follows a specific sign convention. A positive 90° rotation about the Z-axis produces $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$, not its transpose. Mixing up the direction costs full marks. > > 3. **Forgetting Normalization:** In homogeneous coordinates, the final pixel coordinates must be obtained by dividing by the scale factor $s = Z_c$. Skipping this step gives wrong answers. > > 4. **Confusing $(x, y)$ with $(u, v)$:** Image coordinates $(x, y)$ are continuous (in mm), while pixel coordinates $(u, v)$ are discrete (in pixels). The principal point $(c_x, c_y)$ and scaling factors $(\alpha, \beta)$ are essential for the conversion. > > 5. **Incorrect Extrinsic Matrix Size:** $[R \vert t]$ is $3 \times 4$, not $4 \times 4$. The full $4 \times 4$ form $\begin{bmatrix} R & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ is used only for homogeneous point transformation. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pinhole Camera Model** **Core Concepts:** - ☐ The pinhole camera model is the **simplest mathematical model** of image formation in computer vision. - ☐ It uses **perspective projection** based on similar triangles. - ☐ Light passes through a single point (pinhole/optical center) to form an **inverted image** on the image plane. - ☐ The **focal length $f$** determines magnification: $x = f \cdot X/Z$, $y = f \cdot Y/Z$. **Parameter Types:** - ☐ **Intrinsic parameters ($K$):** Focal length, principal point, skew — internal to the camera. - ☐ **Extrinsic parameters ($R, t$):** Rotation and translation — define camera pose in the world. **Key Matrices:** - ☐ **Intrinsic Matrix:** $K = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha & \gamma & c_x \\ 0 & \beta & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ - ☐ **Extrinsic Matrix:** $[R \vert t]$ ($3 \times 4$) - ☐ **Projection Matrix:** $P = K \cdot [R \vert t]$ ($3 \times 4$) **Coordinate Systems:** - ☐ **World coordinates** $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$ → Camera coordinates $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ → Image coordinates $(x, y)$ → Pixel coordinates $(u, v)$. **Critical Formulas to Memorize:** - ☐ Perspective projection: $u = \alpha \cdot X/Z + c_x$, $v = \beta \cdot Y/Z + c_y$ - ☐ Homogeneous projection: $s \cdot [u, v, 1]^T = P \cdot [X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$ - ☐ Scale factor: $s = Z_c$ (always normalize at the end!) **Common Exam Pitfalls:** - ☐ Always convert units consistently (mm vs. meters vs. pixels). - ☐ Always normalize by the depth $s$ in homogeneous coordinates. - ☐ Remember that the image is inverted — mathematically handled by a virtual image plane. - ☐ The rotation matrix $R$ must be orthogonal: $R^T R = I$.

topics.title: Stereo Vision: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT831 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stereo Vision: Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stereo Vision (Stereopsis / Stereoscopic Vision)** is a sub-field of computer vision that aims to recover the three-dimensional (3D) geometric structure of a scene from two or more digital images captured from different viewpoints, mimicking the biological process of human binocular vision.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OEITT831 – Module 2), stereo vision is the process of **extracting 3D depth information from 2D images** by exploiting the **parallax (disparity)** created when the same scene is observed from two or more camera positions.

### Key Terminology
- **Epipolar Geometry:** The geometry of stereo vision that describes the relationship between two cameras observing the same 3D scene.
- **Correspondence Problem:** The task of matching a point in one image to the corresponding point in another image.
- **Disparity:** The apparent shift in position of a 3D point when projected onto the left and right image planes.
- **Depth Map:** A 2D array where each pixel stores the distance from the camera to the corresponding point in the scene.
- **Triangulation:** The geometric method of determining a 3D point's location using the intersection of two rays from known camera positions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stereoscopic Reconstruction Pipeline:** Image Acquisition → Camera Calibration → Rectification → Feature Detection → Correspondence Matching → Disparity Computation → Depth Estimation → 3D Reconstruction

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a busy street and you close one eye. You will notice that judging the exact distance of an approaching car becomes harder. Now, open both eyes. Suddenly, you can perceive depth with remarkable accuracy. **Why?**

Because your two eyes (left and right) are separated by approximately **6.5 cm** (the *interocular distance*). Each eye captures a slightly different view of the same scene. The brain fuses these two images and uses the **subtle horizontal shift** (parallax) of objects between the two views to compute distance. Closer objects shift more, distant objects shift less.

**Stereo vision in computer vision is the engineering replication of this biological phenomenon:**

| Biological System | Computer Vision System |
|---|---|
| Two human eyes | Two calibrated cameras |
| Optic nerve | Image sensor (CCD/CMOS) |
| Visual cortex | Correspondence matching algorithm |
| Brain's depth perception | Triangulation math producing a depth map |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the transition from *image formation* (Module 1) to *multi-view geometry* (Module 2). Stereo vision is the gateway application that connects camera calibration with 3D scene understanding — appearing frequently in **Part B (14-mark)** questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

The stereo vision workflow can be decomposed into the following logical stages:

1. **Image Acquisition (Data Capture):**
   - Two cameras (or one camera translated along a baseline) capture overlapping views of the scene.
   - The baseline $B$ is the distance between the two camera centers.
   - The cameras must have **parallel optical axes** (after rectification) for simplified geometry.

2. **Camera Calibration:**
   - Determines the **intrinsic parameters** (focal length $f$, principal point $(c_x, c_y)$, pixel size) and **extrinsic parameters** (relative rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$ between the two cameras).
   - Critical for metric (real-world) depth recovery.

3. **Image Rectification:**
   - Reprojects both images onto a common plane parallel to the baseline so that corresponding points lie on the same horizontal scanline.
   - Reduces the correspondence problem from 2D search to 1D search.

4. **Feature Detection & Matching (Correspondence):**
   - Algorithms (e.g., Harris corners, SIFT, SURF, or pixel-based SAD/SSD/NCC) find matching pixels/regions in left and right images.
   - Output: A **disparity map** $d(u,v)$.

5. **Depth Computation via Triangulation:**
   - Disparity $d$ is converted to depth $Z$ using the pinhole model.
   - Closer points $\Rightarrow$ larger disparity; farther points $\Rightarrow$ smaller disparity.

6. **3D Reconstruction:**
   - Depth map is optionally combined with camera poses to produce a 3D point cloud or mesh.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage

- **Why two cameras?** A single camera loses depth information during projection (a 3D point collapses to a 2D pixel). A second viewpoint breaks this ambiguity through parallax.
- **Why rectification?** Reduces computational cost from $O(N^2)$ (2D search) to $O(N)$ (1D scanline search).
- **Why calibration?** Without knowing the baseline $B$ and focal length $f$, disparity can only give *relative* depth, not *absolute* metric depth.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield items. Memorize units and the inverse-proportionality relationship.**

| Symbol | Definition | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| $B$ | Baseline (distance between camera centers) | meters (m) |
| $f$ | Focal length of the camera | pixels or mm |
| $Z$ | Depth (perpendicular distance from camera to 3D point) | meters (m) |
| $d$ | Disparity $\equiv x_L - x_R$ | pixels |
| $(u_L, v)$ | Pixel coordinates in left image | pixels |
| $(u_R, v)$ | Pixel coordinates in right image | pixels |
| $\mathbf{K}$ | Camera intrinsic matrix $\begin{bmatrix}f & 0 & c_x \\ 0 & f & c_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$ | 3$\times$3 matrix |
| $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{t}$ | Rotation and translation between cameras | SO(3), $\mathbb{R}^3$ |

### Core Equations

**1. Pinhole Projection (Single Camera)**
$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{K} \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{R} \mid \mathbf{t} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ Z \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**2. Stereo Depth Equation (Rectified Setup)**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**3. Disparity Definition**
$$d = u_L - u_R$$

**4. 3D Reconstruction (per matched pixel)**
$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}, \qquad Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

**5. Baseline-Depield-of-View Trade-off**
$$Z_{\max} = \frac{f \cdot B}{d_{\min}}, \qquad Z_{\min} = \frac{f \cdot B}{d_{\max}}$$

where $d_{\min} \geq 1$ pixel (lowest measurable disparity) and $d_{\max}$ is set by image width.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Inverse Relationship:** $Z \propto 1/d$. If disparity doubles, depth halves. This is the single most important relationship in stereo vision and is the basis of nearly every 14-mark derivation question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Obstacle detection, lane perception, and free-space estimation (e.g., stereo rigs on Toyota, Subaru research platforms).
- **Robotics:** Pick-and-place arms using 3D scene understanding; SLAM front-ends.
- **Medical Imaging:** 3D endoscopy and microsurgery navigation.
- **Augmented Reality (AR):** Depth-aware occlusion and physically plausible object placement.
- **Industrial Metrology:** Quality inspection and reverse engineering using structured-light stereo.
- **Photogrammetry & Remote Sensing:** Generating Digital Elevation Models (DEMs) from aerial image pairs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Depth from Disparity (Standard Rectified Stereo)

**Setup:** Two identical pinhole cameras with focal length $f$ and baseline $B$. Optical axes are parallel and perpendicular to the baseline. Both cameras have the same principal point $(c_x, c_y)$. The world coordinate system origin is at the left camera's optical center.

**Step 1 — Project a 3D point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ onto the left image plane.**

Using similar triangles formed by the optical center, the image plane, and the world point:

$$\frac{u_L - c_x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z}$$

Solving for the left image horizontal coordinate:

$$u_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x$$

**Step 2 — Project the same 3D point onto the right image plane.**

The right camera is translated by $-B$ along the X-axis relative to the world frame. The world coordinate of $P$ as seen from the right camera is $(X - B, Y, Z)$. Thus:

$$u_R = f \cdot \frac{(X - B)}{Z} + c_x$$

**Step 3 — Compute the disparity $d$.**

$$d = u_L - u_R = \left( f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} + c_x \right) - \left( f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} + c_x \right)$$

The $c_x$ terms cancel:

$$d = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z} - \frac{f \cdot (X - B)}{Z}$$

$$d = \frac{f \cdot X - f \cdot X + f \cdot B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Final depth equation.**

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Disparity combines camera intrinsics ($f$) and extrinsics ($B$) scaled by the inverse of scene depth. Since $f$ and $B$ are constants for a fixed rig, the *only variable* is $d$, which is the measurable quantity from images.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity and Depth Resolution

**Goal:** Quantify the error in depth $\Delta Z$ given a sub-pixel disparity error $\Delta d$.

Starting from $Z = fB / d$, take the differential:

$$dZ = -\frac{fB}{d^2} \, dd = -\frac{Z^2}{fB} \, dd$$

Taking absolute values:

$$\vert \Delta Z \vert = \frac{Z^2}{fB} \cdot \vert \Delta d \vert$$

**Interpretation:** Depth error grows quadratically with distance. For a fixed rig, halving the baseline doubles the depth error; doubling the focal length halves the depth error.

## 3.3 Derivation: 3D World Coordinates from a Matched Pixel Pair

Given a matched pixel $(u_L, v)$ in the left image and $(u_R, v)$ in the right image (rectified, so $v$ is identical), reconstruct $(X, Y, Z)$ in the left camera frame.

**Step 1 — Compute disparity.**
$$d = u_L - u_R$$

**Step 2 — Compute depth.**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Compute X using the left projection equation.**

$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot Z}{f}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Y using the vertical projection equation.**

$$Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot Z}{f}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d} \\
X &= \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot B}{d} \\
Y &= \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot B}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the principal point cancels for Z but not X, Y?** The depth equation $Z = fB/d$ depends only on the *difference* $u_L - u_R$, so the common $c_x$ cancels. The X coordinate, however, depends on the absolute pixel position $(u_L - c_x)$, so $c_x$ is retained.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A stereo rig has focal length $f = 800$ pixels and baseline $B = 0.12$ m. A matched point pair has $u_L = 412$ and $u_R = 396$ on scanline $v = 250$ (principal point $c_x = 320$, $c_y = 240$). Compute the 3D world coordinates.

**Step 1 — Disparity.**
$$d = u_L - u_R = 412 - 396 = 16 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2 — Depth.**
$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{96}{16} = 6.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — X coordinate.**
$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(412 - 320) \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{92 \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{11.04}{16} = 0.69 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Y coordinate.**
$$Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(250 - 240) \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{10 \times 0.12}{16} = \frac{1.2}{16} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Final 3D Point:** $P = (0.69 \text{ m},\ 0.075 \text{ m},\ 6.0 \text{ m})$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to **subtract the principal point** when computing $X$ and $Y$. The depth formula has it cancel, but $X$ and $Y$ do not. See Section 5 for examiner warnings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Stereo Vision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene and Lighting] --> B[Left Camera Capture]
    A --> C[Right Camera Capture]
    B --> D[Left Image IL]
    C --> E[Right Image IR]
    D --> F[Camera Calibration K R t]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Image Rectification]
    G --> H[Left Rectified Image]
    G --> I[Right Rectified Image]
    H --> J[Feature Detection Corners SIFT]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Correspondence Matching]
    K --> L[Disparity Map d u v]
    L --> M[Triangulation using Z fB over d]
    M --> N[Depth Map Z u v]
    N --> O[3D Point Cloud X Y Z]
    O --> P[3D Surface Reconstruction Mesh]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The pipeline is a directed acyclic graph. Each block transforms its input into a more geometrically meaningful representation. The **rectification** block is what enables the 1D scanline search that makes real-time stereo possible.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Geometric Setup (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StereoRIG[Parallel Stereo Rig]
        CAML[Left Camera at origin O_L]
        CAMR[Right Camera at O_R on X axis]
    end
    SCENE[3D World Point P at X Y Z]
    CAML -->|ray 1 at angle theta L| SCENE
    CAMR -->|ray 2 at angle theta R| SCENE
    BL[Baseline B between O_L and O_R]
    CAML -.-> BL
    BL -.-> CAMR
```

**Geometric Reading:**
- The 3D point $P$ is observed along ray-1 from the left camera and ray-2 from the right camera.
- The two rays intersect at $P$ — this is the **triangulation principle**.
- After rectification, the image planes are coplanar, so the search for correspondence is restricted to a single horizontal scanline.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Disparity vs. Depth Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OBJ_NEAR[Close Object Z small] --> D_LARGE[Large Disparity d large]
    OBJ_FAR[Far Object Z large] --> D_SMALL[Small Disparity d small]
    D_LARGE --> EQ[Z equals fB over d]
    D_SMALL --> EQ
    EQ --> INV[Inverse Proportionality Z alpha 1 over d]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mental Model for Exams:** Always draw the **inverse relationship curve** (hyperbola) for $Z$ vs. $d$. Examiners award partial credit even when a diagram is crude, as long as the axes are correctly labeled.

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Error Propagation Sources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC1[Camera Calibration Error] --> ERR[Depth Error]
    SRC2[Subpixel Quantization in Matching] --> ERR
    SRC3[Lens Distortion not modeled] --> ERR
    SRC4[Occlusions and Textureless Regions] --> ERR
    SRC5[Baseline Uncertainty] --> ERR
    ERR --> OUT[Erroneous 3D Point Cloud]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define stereo vision. What is the biological inspiration behind it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stereo vision (stereopsis) is a computer vision technique that recovers the 3D structure of a scene from two or more 2D images captured from different viewpoints by exploiting the **parallax (disparity)** between corresponding points. *(1.5 marks)*

The biological inspiration is **human binocular vision**: our two eyes, separated by approximately **6.5 cm (interocular distance)**, capture slightly different views of the same scene. The brain fuses these two images and uses the horizontal shift (parallax) of objects to compute depth — closer objects shift more, distant objects shift less. *(1.5 marks)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is meant by the correspondence problem in stereo vision? Why is image rectification used to simplify it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **correspondence problem** is the task of identifying which pixel (or feature) in the left image corresponds to the same physical 3D point in the right image. Solving it is the core computational challenge of stereo vision. *(1.5 marks)*

**Image rectification** reprojects both images onto a common plane parallel to the baseline, so that corresponding points lie on the same horizontal scanline. This reduces the correspondence search from a 2D search (the entire image) to a 1D search (along a single row), drastically reducing computation and eliminating false matches. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget the principal point** $(c_x, c_y)$ when computing $X$ and $Y$ coordinates, even though it cancels in the depth formula. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
> - Students often write the depth equation as $Z = fB/d$ without specifying the assumptions (rectified cameras, parallel optical axes, identical intrinsics). Always state assumptions explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - Numerical answers must carry **units** (m, pixels, etc.). A correct number without units is considered incomplete. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> - For derivations, examiners expect a **diagram** before the math. A rough sketch showing baseline, optical centers, and the 3D point earns easy credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Stereo Geometry & Depth Computation

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, derive the relationship between depth $Z$, focal length $f$, baseline $B$, and disparity $d$ in a rectified parallel stereo vision system. State clearly all assumptions. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Cameras are identical pinhole cameras with the same focal length $f$ and same principal point $(c_x, c_y)$.
2. Optical axes are parallel and perpendicular to the baseline $B$.
3. Image planes are coplanar after rectification.
4. Lenses are distortion-free.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Top-down view showing left camera at origin $O_L$, right camera at $O_R$ translated by $B$ along the X-axis, image planes, and 3D point $P$ projecting to $(u_L, v)$ and $(u_R, v)$.

**Derivation (4 marks):**
From similar triangles at the left camera:
$$\frac{u_L - c_x}{f} = \frac{X}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_L = \frac{fX}{Z} + c_x$$

From the right camera (world point relative to right frame is $(X - B, Y, Z)$):
$$\frac{u_R - c_x}{f} = \frac{X - B}{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_R = \frac{f(X - B)}{Z} + c_x$$

Subtracting:
$$d = u_L - u_R = \frac{fX}{Z} - \frac{f(X - B)}{Z} = \frac{fB}{Z}$$

$$\boxed{Z = \frac{fB}{d}}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Depth is inversely proportional to disparity. Closer objects produce larger disparities, distant objects produce smaller ones. This is the foundational equation of stereo vision.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** A stereo camera rig has $f = 1200$ pixels and $B = 0.20$ m. For a given scene point, the disparity is measured as 24 pixels. Compute the depth. If the baseline is doubled to 0.40 m, what is the new disparity for the same point, assuming the camera and scene are unchanged? *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Compute depth (3 marks):**
$$Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{1200 \times 0.20}{24} = \frac{240}{24} = 10.0 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii) — New disparity after doubling baseline (4 marks):**

For the same 3D point, depth $Z$ and focal length $f$ remain constant. The disparity equation rearranges to:
$$d = \frac{fB}{Z}$$

With the new baseline $B' = 2B = 0.40$ m:
$$d' = \frac{fB'}{Z} = \frac{1200 \times 0.40}{10.0} = \frac{480}{10.0} = 48 \text{ pixels}$$

**Verification using the proportionality (1 mark):**
$$d' = d \times \frac{B'}{B} = 24 \times \frac{0.40}{0.20} = 48 \text{ pixels} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** Doubling the baseline doubles the disparity for the same scene point, which improves depth resolution at the cost of a smaller overlapping field of view.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice: Correspondence & 3D Reconstruction

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a)** Explain the stereo vision pipeline. Describe the role of camera calibration, rectification, and correspondence matching. List two algorithms used for correspondence matching. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Stereo Vision Pipeline (3 marks):**

The end-to-end pipeline consists of the following stages:
1. **Image Acquisition:** Two cameras capture overlapping views of the scene with a known baseline $B$.
2. **Camera Calibration:** Determines intrinsic parameters ($f$, $c_x$, $c_y$, distortion coefficients) and extrinsic parameters (relative rotation $\mathbf{R}$ and translation $\mathbf{t}$). Essential for metric reconstruction.
3. **Image Rectification:** Reprojects both images so that corresponding points lie on the same horizontal scanline, reducing the correspondence search to 1D.
4. **Feature Detection & Correspondence:** Finds matching pixels/regions in left and right images; outputs a disparity map.
5. **Depth Computation:** Applies $Z = fB/d$ at every pixel to obtain a depth map.
6. **3D Reconstruction:** Combines depth with image coordinates to generate a 3D point cloud.

**Role of Key Stages (3 marks):**
- **Calibration** converts disparity into absolute (metric) depth; without it, only relative depth is computable.
- **Rectification** reduces the 2D correspondence search to 1D (scanline-based), making real-time stereo computationally feasible.
- **Correspondence matching** is the actual computational engine — its accuracy directly determines depth accuracy.

**Algorithms (1 mark — list any two):**
1. **Block Matching (SAD/SSD/NCC):** Pixel-based, fast, sensitive to lighting changes.
2. **Semi-Global Matching (SGM):** Pixel-based with smoothness constraints, robust, widely used in ADAS.
3. **SIFT/SURF feature matching:** Scale-invariant, sparse, robust to rotation and scale.
4. **Deep learning stereo networks (e.g., PSMNet, RAFT-Stereo):** End-to-end learned, state-of-the-art accuracy.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b)** Given the left image pixel $(u_L, v) = (520, 310)$ and the corresponding right image pixel $(u_R, v) = (488, 310)$ from a rectified stereo rig with $f = 1000$ pixels, $B = 0.15$ m, principal point $(c_x, c_y) = (400, 300)$, reconstruct the 3D world coordinates $(X, Y, Z)$ of the corresponding scene point. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute disparity (1 mark):**
$$d = u_L - u_R = 520 - 488 = 32 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 2 — Compute depth Z (2 marks):**
$$Z = \frac{fB}{d} = \frac{1000 \times 0.15}{32} = \frac{150}{32} = 4.6875 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute X (2 marks):**
$$X = \frac{(u_L - c_x) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(520 - 400) \times 0.15}{32} = \frac{120 \times 0.15}{32} = \frac{18}{32} = 0.5625 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Y (1 mark):**
$$Y = \frac{(v - c_y) \cdot B}{d} = \frac{(310 - 300) \times 0.15}{32} = \frac{10 \times 0.15}{32} = \frac{1.5}{32} = 0.046875 \text{ m}$$

**Final Result (1 mark):**
$$P = (0.5625 \text{ m},\ 0.046875 \text{ m},\ 4.6875 \text{ m})$$

**Verification:** The point lies slightly to the right of the optical axis ($X > 0$), marginally below the principal ray ($Y > 0$), and at approximately 4.69 m depth — all physically consistent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- ⭐ **Core Equation:** $Z = fB / d$ — inverse proportionality is the *single most testable* relationship.
- ⭐ **Disparity Definition:** $d = u_L - u_R$ (always left minus right in the standard convention).
- ⭐ **Biological Analogy:** Human binocular vision; interocular distance $\approx$ **6.5 cm**.
- ⭐ **Pipeline Order:** Acquisition → Calibration → Rectification → Correspondence → Disparity → Depth → 3D.
- ⭐ **Rectification Purpose:** Reduces 2D correspondence search to 1D (scanline-based).
- ⭐ **Principal Point Role:** Cancels in $Z$, but **must** be subtracted when computing $X$ and $Y$.
- ⭐ **Baseline Trade-off:** Larger $B$ → better depth resolution, smaller overlapping FOV; smaller $B$ → larger FOV, poorer depth resolution.
- ⭐ **Depth Error Formula:** $\vert \Delta Z \vert = (Z^2 / fB) \cdot \vert \Delta d \vert$ — error grows **quadratically** with distance.
- ⭐ **Matching Algorithms:** Block Matching (SAD/SSD), Semi-Global Matching (SGM), feature-based (SIFT/SURF), deep stereo networks.
- ⭐ **Sources of Error:** Calibration error, subpixel quantization, lens distortion, occlusions, textureless regions.
- ⭐ **Real-World Applications:** Autonomous vehicles, robotics pick-and-place, medical endoscopy, AR depth occlusion, photogrammetry DEMs.
- ⭐ **Always state assumptions** in derivations: parallel optical axes, identical intrinsics, pinhole model, distortion-free lenses.
- ⭐ **Always carry units** in numerical answers: pixels for $f, d, u, v, c_x, c_y$; meters for $B, X, Y, Z$.
- ⭐ **Draw a diagram first** in any 14-mark derivation question — even a rough sketch earns credit.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any stereo vision problem on the KTU 2024 ESE, identify whether the question is asking for (i) the depth equation derivation, (ii) a numerical depth/XYZ computation, or (iii) a pipeline description. Each has a fixed answer template — learn the structure, not just the facts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Disparity and Its Relationship with Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT831 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of Disparity and Its Relationship with Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **stereo vision (stereopsis)**, the **Concept of Disparity** refers to the apparent positional shift of a corresponding 3-D scene point when it is projected onto the image planes of two cameras (or two eyes) that are spatially separated by a fixed horizontal distance called the **baseline**. Mathematically, disparity is defined as the difference in the image-plane horizontal coordinates of the same physical point when observed from the **left image** and the **right image**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disparity (d)** is formally given by $d = x_L - x_R$, where $x_L$ and $x_R$ are the horizontal pixel (or metric) coordinates of the projection of a common 3-D world point on the left and right rectified image planes, respectively.

The **Depth (Z)** of a scene point is its perpendicular distance measured along the optical axis from the camera's projection center. In a calibrated stereo rig, **disparity and depth are inversely proportional**: greater disparity corresponds to a closer object, while smaller disparity corresponds to a more distant object.

> [!NOTE]
> A **disparity map** is a 2-D image in which every pixel stores the disparity value $d(x,y)$ computed for that pixel through correspondence (matching) between the stereo pair.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding your index finger at arm's length and alternately closing your left and right eye. The finger appears to *jump* horizontally against the background. Now move the finger closer to your face and repeat — the jump becomes noticeably larger. This horizontal jump is exactly **disparity**. The brain uses this displacement to estimate **how far** the finger is from your eyes.

**Real-world engineering analogue:** Two CCTV cameras mounted on a pillar at a toll booth capture a passing vehicle. Because the cameras are physically separated, the same license plate sits at slightly different pixel positions in the two video frames. By measuring how far apart those pixels are, the embedded computer instantly calculates the vehicle's distance — this is the **disparity-to-depth pipeline** in action.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bold-Highlighted)

- **Baseline (B):** The horizontal distance between the two camera optical centers. Standard values range from **6 cm** (human inter-ocular distance) to several meters in robotic/automotive stereo rigs.
- **Focal Length (f):** The perpendicular distance from the lens/pinhole to the image plane, measured in **pixels or millimeters**.
- **Depth (Z):** Perpendicular distance from the baseline to the scene point, measured in the **same world unit** as the baseline.
- **Disparity (d):** Apparent horizontal shift, expressed in **pixels** after rectification.

> [!TIP]
> Remember the **3 B's of Stereo**: **B**aseline, **B**oth cameras, and **B**oth image planes. If any one is missing, disparity cannot be computed reliably.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Why Disparity Exists

Stereo depth recovery is rooted in **triangulation**, the same geometric principle surveyors use to measure mountain heights. The reasoning unfolds in structured stages:

- **Stage 1 — Two Pinhole Projections:** A 3-D world point $P$ projects onto both image planes because light travels in straight lines through each camera's pinhole (or lens center).
- **Stage 2 — Coordinate Shift:** Because the right camera is shifted horizontally by $B$, the projection of $P$ lands at a *different* horizontal coordinate on the right image plane. Vertically, the projections coincide (after **rectification**).
- **Stage 3 — Disparity Measurement:** The horizontal shift is quantified as $d = x_L - x_R$.
- **Stage 4 — Depth Inference:** From similar triangles formed by the optical rays, depth is recovered using the inverse relationship $Z = fB/d$.

## 2.2 Assumptions of the Standard Disparity Model

For the canonical formula $Z = fB/d$ to hold exactly, the stereo rig must satisfy:

1. **Rectification:** Both image planes are coplanar and their rows are perfectly aligned (no vertical parallax).
2. **Equal Focal Lengths:** $f_L = f_R = f$.
3. **Parallel Optical Axes:** Both cameras look straight ahead with no relative rotation.
4. **Known Baseline:** The baseline $B$ has been calibrated.
5. **Perfect Correspondence:** Each pixel in the left image has exactly one matching pixel in the right image.

> [!WARNING]
> Real-world setups almost never satisfy all these conditions perfectly. **Stereo rectification** and **camera calibration** (using checkerboard patterns and intrinsic/extrinsic parameters) are mandatory preprocessing steps in any production-grade stereo system.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = x_L - x_R$ | $d$: disparity (px), $x_L, x_R$: image coordinates | Stereo matching output |
| 2 | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{d}$ | $Z$: depth (m), $f$: focal length (px), $B$: baseline (m) | Primary depth-recovery equation |
| 3 | $d_{min} = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z_{max}}$ | $d_{min}$: smallest detectable disparity | Sets far-range limit of stereo rig |
| 4 | $d_{max} = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{Z_{min}}$ | $d_{max}$: largest disparity before occlusion | Sets near-range limit |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z^2} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{f \cdot B}$ | Depth error from disparity quantization | Sensitivity analysis |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{Z} = \dfrac{d}{f \cdot B}$ | Inverse-depth form (linear in $d$) | Optimization & learning pipelines |
| 7 | $x_L = f \cdot \dfrac{X}{Z}$ | Left-camera perspective projection | Derivation anchor |
| 8 | $x_R = f \cdot \dfrac{X-B}{Z}$ | Right-camera perspective projection | Derivation anchor |

> [!NOTE]
> Note the use of $\dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z^2}$ instead of the vertical pipe symbol. The depth error grows *quadratically* with distance — at twice the depth, disparity uncertainty translates to four times the depth uncertainty. This is why stereo systems struggle at long range.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Disparity-to-depth recovery is foundational in:

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time obstacle ranging using forward-facing stereo cameras.
- **Robotic Manipulation:** Bin picking and assembly-line grasping.
- **Medical Imaging:** Endoscopic stereo for minimally invasive surgery.
- **AR/VR:** Spatial mapping for immersive experiences.
- **Photogrammetry & Remote Sensing:** Aerial stereo pairs for terrain elevation models (DEMs).
- **Industrial Metrology:** Sub-millimeter measurement using calibrated stereo rigs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Depth-from-Disparity Equation

We begin with two cameras modeled as ideal pinhole cameras. The optical axes are parallel, both pointing in the $+Z$ direction of the world frame. The right camera's center is shifted by $+B$ along the world's $X$-axis relative to the left camera's center.

Let $P = (X, Y, Z)$ be the 3-D world coordinates of a point of interest. The vertical coordinate $Y$ is irrelevant for disparity in a rectified rig, so we focus on the $(X, Z)$ plane.

**Step 1 — Projection onto the Left Image Plane**

By the pinhole projection equation (similar triangles), the horizontal image coordinate of $P$ in the left camera is given by:

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

This is derived because the ray from $P$ through the left optical center makes an angle with the optical axis such that $\tan(\theta_L) = X/Z$, and the image-plane intercept is $f \cdot \tan(\theta_L) = fX/Z$.

**Step 2 — Projection onto the Right Image Plane**

The right optical center sits at world coordinate $X = B$. Therefore the horizontal displacement of $P$ *relative to the right camera* is $(X - B)$. Applying the same pinhole relation to the right camera:

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Step 3 — Define Disparity**

Disparity is the difference of the two image coordinates (with the left image shifted relative to the right when the point is in front of the cameras):

$$d = x_L - x_R$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Expressions**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\
&= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} \;-\; f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
&= \frac{f}{Z} \cdot \left[ X - (X - B) \right] \\
&= \frac{f}{Z} \cdot B
\end{aligned}$$

The cancellation of $X$ is the geometric essence of triangulation: regardless of where the point lies laterally, the *difference* in projections depends only on depth $Z$, baseline $B$, and focal length $f$.

**Step 5 — Solve for Depth**

Rearranging the expression $d = fB/Z$ for $Z$:

$$\begin{aligned}
d \cdot Z &= f \cdot B \\
Z &= \frac{f \cdot B}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **master equation of stereo depth recovery**.

## 3.2 Inverse-Depth Form

Because disparity is inversely proportional to depth, depth is non-linear in disparity. For computational pipelines (especially optimization-based learning), it is often convenient to work in **inverse depth**, which is linear in disparity:

$$\frac{1}{Z} = \frac{d}{f \cdot B}$$

This linear form is exploited in SLAM, bundle adjustment, and modern self-supervised depth networks.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A stereo camera rig has a baseline of $B = 0.12$ m and a focal length of $f = 800$ pixels (after pixel-scaling). The stereo matching algorithm reports a disparity of $d = 40$ pixels at a particular pixel. Compute the depth of the corresponding scene point.

**Solution:**

Step (i) — Identify the knowns:

$$f = 800 \text{ px}, \quad B = 0.12 \text{ m}, \quad d = 40 \text{ px}$$

Step (ii) — Apply the master equation:

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{800 \cdot 0.12}{40}$$

Step (iii) — Evaluate the numerator:

$$800 \cdot 0.12 = 96.0$$

Step (iv) — Divide:

$$Z = \frac{96.0}{40} = 2.4 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** The scene point is **2.4 meters** away from the stereo rig along the optical axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note that $f$ is in **pixels** and $B$ is in **meters**. The resulting $Z$ emerges in **meters** because the conversion factor between pixel units and world units is embedded in $f$ when it is expressed in pixels (i.e., $f$ already accounts for the sensor's pixel pitch).

## 3.4 Sensitivity Analysis — How Depth Error Reacts to Disparity Error

Real disparity estimates have sub-pixel quantization noise $\Delta d$. The resulting depth error $\Delta Z$ is obtained by differentiating the master equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z &= f \cdot B \cdot d^{-1} \\
\frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} &= -f \cdot B \cdot d^{-2} \\
\Delta Z &\approx \vert \frac{\partial Z}{\partial d} \vert \cdot \Delta d \\
&= \frac{f \cdot B}{d^2} \cdot \Delta d
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $Z = fB/d$, we can rewrite the depth error as:

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$$

**Insight:** Depth error grows as the **square of depth**. If you double the distance to the object, the same disparity noise produces **four times** the depth uncertainty. This is the principal reason why stereo systems are reliable at close range and unreliable at long range.

## 3.5 Worked Sensitivity Example

Given $f = 800$ px, $B = 0.12$ m, $Z = 2.4$ m, and a disparity uncertainty of $\Delta d = 0.5$ px:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d \\
&= \frac{(2.4)^2}{800 \cdot 0.12} \cdot 0.5 \\
&= \frac{5.76}{96} \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 0.06 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 0.03 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A half-pixel disparity noise produces only **3 cm of depth uncertainty** at 2.4 m. Excellent accuracy for close-range applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Binocular Stereo Geometry — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the geometric and computational architecture of a binocular stereo rig in functional form, capturing the data flow from world point acquisition to disparity-map and depth-map outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Point P at X Y Z] --> B[Left Pinhole Camera]
    A --> C[Right Pinhole Camera Shifted by Baseline B]
    B --> D[Left Image Plane at xL f times X over Z]
    C --> E[Right Image Plane at xR f times X minus B over Z]
    D --> F[Rectification Stage Row Alignment]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Stereo Matching Engine]
    G --> H[Disparity Map d equals xL minus xR]
    H --> I[Depth Calculator Z equals fB over d]
    I --> J[Depth Map Z per Pixel]
    J --> K[Application Layer Obstacle Avoidance or 3D Reconstruction]
```

## 4.2 Disparity-to-Depth Processing Pipeline

The next diagram captures the complete algorithmic pipeline from raw image capture to usable depth information. It highlights the mandatory preprocessing stages (calibration and rectification) that enable the canonical formula $Z = fB/d$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Raw Left Image] --> stepB[Camera Calibration Intrinsic Matrix]
    stepC[Raw Right Image] --> stepB
    stepB --> stepD[Stereo Rectification Epipolar Alignment]
    stepD --> stepE[Feature or Pixel Matching]
    stepE --> stepF[Disparity Computation d equals xL minus xR]
    stepF --> stepG[Disparity Map]
    stepG --> stepH[Depth Recovery Z equals fB over d]
    stepH --> stepI[Depth Map or Point Cloud]
    stepI --> stepJ[Postprocessing Median Filter and Hole Filling]
    stepJ --> stepK[Downstream Application]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Disparity, Baseline, Focal Length, and Depth

This conceptual block-diagram illustrates the inverse and proportional relationships between the four core stereo quantities, which is the most common KTU examination question type.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Focal Length f] --> n3[Disparity d]
    n2[Baseline B] --> n3
    n3 --> n4[Depth Z]
    n1 --> n4
    n2 --> n4
    n4 --> n5[Inverse Proportionality Quadratic Sensitivity]
    n3 --> n5
    n5 --> n6[Close Range High Accuracy]
    n5 --> n7[Long Range Degraded Accuracy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams intentionally abstract the physical 3-D geometry into functional blocks because Mermaid cannot render true 3-D projective geometry. In a classroom setting, these should be paired with a hand-drawn pinhole-model sketch showing the two converging rays and the similar-triangle relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define disparity in stereo vision. Explain how it relates to the depth of a scene point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Disparity (1 mark):** Disparity is the difference in the horizontal image-plane coordinates of the same 3-D world point when projected onto the left and right cameras of a stereo rig, mathematically expressed as $d = x_L - x_R$.

**Relationship with Depth (2 marks):** Disparity and depth are inversely related through the equation $Z = fB/d$, where $f$ is the focal length and $B$ is the baseline. As depth $Z$ increases, disparity $d$ decreases hyperbolically. A point closer to the camera produces a larger disparity; a point farther away produces a smaller disparity. This inverse relationship forms the foundation of stereo depth recovery via triangulation.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is a disparity map? Mention any two applications where disparity maps are used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A disparity map is a 2-D grayscale image in which the intensity (or stored value) at each pixel represents the disparity value $d(x,y)$ computed for that pixel between the left and right stereo images, typically after rectification. Brighter pixels often denote closer objects (higher disparity) and darker pixels denote distant objects (lower disparity).

**Applications (1.5 marks):**
1. **Autonomous vehicle perception** — obstacle detection and free-space estimation.
2. **3-D scene reconstruction** — generation of dense point clouds for AR/VR and photogrammetry.
3. (Optional credit) Robot navigation in unstructured environments.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Derive the relationship between disparity and depth for a parallel stereo camera configuration. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Both cameras are pinhole cameras with equal focal lengths $f_L = f_R = f$.
2. The optical axes are parallel and both cameras look along the same $+Z$ direction.
3. The image planes are coplanar and rectified — there is no vertical disparity.
4. The baseline $B$ between the optical centers is known and lies along the world $X$-axis.
5. Intrinsic and extrinsic parameters are known (camera calibration performed).

**Setup (1 mark):** Let the world point be $P = (X, Y, Z)$ with the right camera's optical center at $X = B$ relative to the left camera at $X = 0$.

**Left Image Projection (1 mark):** By the pinhole projection model,

$$x_L = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

**Right Image Projection (1 mark):** With the right camera offset by $B$,

$$x_R = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z}$$

**Disparity Definition and Simplification (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= x_L - x_R \\
&= f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} \\
&= \frac{f}{Z}\left[X - (X - B)\right] \\
&= \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Depth Relation (1 mark):** Rearranging,

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d}$$

This is the canonical stereo depth-recovery equation, demonstrating the inverse proportionality between depth and disparity.

---

#### (b) A stereo rig has a baseline of 15 cm and focal length of 600 pixels. The stereo matching reports a disparity of 25 pixels for a scene point. Calculate the depth. If the disparity error is ±0.5 pixels, compute the depth uncertainty. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
$f = 600$ px, $B = 0.15$ m, $d = 25$ px, $\Delta d = 0.5$ px.

**Depth Calculation (2 marks):**

$$Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{d} = \frac{600 \times 0.15}{25} = \frac{90}{25} = 3.6 \text{ m}$$

**Depth Uncertainty Derivation (2 marks):** From the sensitivity relation,

$$\Delta Z = \frac{Z^2}{f \cdot B} \cdot \Delta d$$

**Numerical Evaluation (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= \frac{(3.6)^2}{600 \times 0.15} \times 0.5 \\
&= \frac{12.96}{90} \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.144 \times 0.5 \\
&= 0.072 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** The scene point lies at $Z = 3.6$ m, with a depth uncertainty of $\Delta Z = \pm 7.2$ cm. The disparity-based measurement is reliable for close-range applications but would degrade at greater depths due to the quadratic error growth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the concept of disparity with a suitable diagram. Discuss the inverse relationship between disparity and depth. (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):** Disparity is the apparent horizontal shift of a 3-D world point between the left and right image planes of a stereo camera pair. It arises because the two cameras are separated horizontally by a distance called the baseline. The same point projects to different horizontal pixel positions in the two images, and this difference is the disparity. (Refer to the binocular block diagram from Section 4.1.)

**Geometric Origin (2 marks):** Using similar triangles formed by the optical rays converging on the two cameras, the disparity can be shown to be $d = fB/Z$. The quantity $X$ cancels out, demonstrating that disparity depends only on focal length, baseline, and depth.

**Inverse Relationship (2 marks):** Because $d = fB/Z$, doubling the depth halves the disparity. The relationship is hyperbolic and *not* linear. Practically, this means:
- Nearby objects have large disparities → easily measurable.
- Distant objects have very small disparities → harder to measure accurately.
- Beyond a threshold $d_{min}$, the disparity becomes smaller than one pixel, marking the far-range limit of the rig.

---

#### (b) A forward-looking automotive stereo camera has $B = 0.20$ m, $f = 1000$ px. (i) Compute the disparity values for objects at 5 m, 10 m, and 50 m. (ii) What is the smallest detectable depth change at 50 m if the disparity resolution is 1 pixel? (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
$f = 1000$ px, $B = 0.20$ m.

**Part (i) — Disparity Values (3 marks):**

For $Z = 5$ m:
$$d = \frac{fB}{Z} = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{5} = 40 \text{ px}$$

For $Z = 10$ m:
$$d = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{10} = 20 \text{ px}$$

For $Z = 50$ m:
$$d = \frac{1000 \times 0.20}{50} = 4 \text{ px}$$

**Part (ii) — Smallest Detectable Depth Change at 50 m (3 marks):**

Using the sensitivity relation with $\Delta d = 1$ px:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= \frac{Z^2}{fB} \cdot \Delta d \\
&= \frac{(50)^2}{1000 \times 0.20} \times 1 \\
&= \frac{2500}{200} \\
&= 12.5 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer:** At 50 m distance, a 1-pixel disparity resolution yields a depth resolution of only $\pm 12.5$ m — clearly insufficient for high-speed driving. This is why long-range automotive perception often supplements stereo with **LiDAR** or **radar**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Most Common Mark Loss Areas):**
> 1. **Forgetting units consistency** — mixing pixel-focal-length with non-converted baseline produces nonsensical depth values.
> 2. **Sign errors in $x_R$** — always subtract $B$ in the right-camera equation because the right camera is shifted in $+X$.
> 3. **Missing the assumption list** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks when derivation assumptions (rectification, parallel axes, equal focal lengths) are not stated.
> 4. **Linearising the inverse relationship incorrectly** — depth is *not* linear in disparity; depth error is *quadratic* in depth.
> 5. **Confusing disparity with optical flow** — disparity is spatial (between cameras at the same instant), while optical flow is temporal (between frames in time).
> 6. **Omitting the cancellation of $X$** — explicitly showing the algebraic cancellation earns full derivation marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Disparity** is the horizontal pixel offset of the same 3-D point between left and right rectified images: $d = x_L - x_R$.
- **Depth** is the perpendicular distance from the baseline plane to the scene point along the optical axis.
- **Master equation:** $Z = fB/d$. Memorise this — it appears in almost every KTU stereo-vision question.
- **Inverse proportionality:** depth $\propto 1/d$. Nearby objects have *large* disparity; distant objects have *small* disparity.
- **Quadratic error growth:** $\Delta Z = Z^2 \Delta d / (fB)$. Doubling depth quadruples the depth error.
- **Pipeline order:** Calibration → Rectification → Matching → Disparity Map → Depth Map → Postprocessing.
- **Assumption checklist:** Parallel optical axes, equal focal lengths, rectified image planes, known baseline.
- **Engineering impact:** The quadratic error is why stereo is ideal for close-range (robotics, AR) and why LiDAR/radar dominates long-range (autonomous highway driving).
- **Numerical tip:** Always express $f$ in *pixels* and $B$ in *metres* — the result then emerges in metres, with the pixel-to-metre conversion baked into $f$ via the sensor's pixel pitch.
- **Inverse-depth form:** $1/Z = d/(fB)$ is linear in $d$ and preferred in modern learning-based depth pipelines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harris Corner Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT831 - COMPUTER VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harris Corner Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harris Corner Detector (Harris & Stephens, 1988)** is a classical corner detection operator in computer vision that identifies points in an image where the **local intensity changes significantly in more than one direction**. Mathematically, it is founded on the analysis of the **Second Moment Matrix (Structure Tensor)** $M$ computed from image intensity gradients within a local window.

A **corner** (or interest point) is formally defined as a pixel location where the **auto-correlation function** of the image patch exhibits a sharp, distinctive peak, indicating that the patch is recognizable even when shifted in multiple directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses the Module 3 objective — *"Feature Detection & Matching"* — specifically the classical gradient-based corner detection paradigm that preceded learned keypoint methods (ORB, SIFT).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing inside a large, plain white room. Now imagine you are standing in the corner of a furnished room where two walls meet a floor, with furniture and pictures all around.

- **Flat region (white wall):** If you shift your gaze slightly in any direction, nothing really changes — the wall still looks the same. The image is *self-similar* under translation. → **No corner detected.**
- **Edge (the line where the wall meets the floor):** If you shift your gaze *along* the edge, it still looks the same. But if you shift *perpendicular* to the edge, things change drastically. → **One direction of change, ONE large eigenvalue → Edge.**
- **Corner (the actual corner of the room):** If you shift your gaze in **any** direction, the scene changes completely. → **Multiple directions of change, TWO large eigenvalues → Corner!**

The Harris detector mathematically quantifies this intuition by examining how image intensity "shifts" under infinitesimal translations $(u, v)$.

## 1.3 Key Properties (Highlighted for KTU Exams)

| Property | Value / Behaviour |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Inventors** | Chris Harris & Mike Stephens |
| **Year** | **1988** |
| **Rotational Invariance** | ✅ **Yes** (uses gradient products) |
| **Scale Invariance** | ❌ **No** (a known limitation) |
| **Affine Invariance** | ❌ **No** |
| **Computational Cost** | Low (only first-order derivatives) |
| **Empirical Constant** | $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ (typically **0.04** or **0.05**) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Concept: Auto-Correlation of Image Patches

The Harris detector formalizes corner detection by measuring the **change in image intensity** when a small local window $W$ is shifted by a displacement $(u, v)$.

Define the **sum of squared differences (SSD)** energy function:

$$
E(u, v) = \sum_{x,y \in W} w(x,y) \, \big[\, I(x+u,\; y+v) - I(x,y) \,\big]^2
$$

where:
- $I(x,y)$ is the **image intensity** at pixel $(x,y)$
- $w(x,y)$ is a **window function** (typically a Gaussian — gives more weight to the center, less to the periphery)
- $(u,v)$ is the **displacement vector**
- $W$ is the local window centered on the candidate point

### 2.1.1 Interpretation of $E(u,v)$
- If $E(u,v)$ is **small for all** $(u,v)$ → patch looks identical under any shift → **flat region**.
- If $E(u,v)$ is **small along one direction** but large along the other → **edge**.
- If $E(u,v)$ is **large in all directions** → **corner**.

## 2.2 Taylor Series Approximation (Linearisation)

For small shifts $(u,v)$, the shifted intensity is approximated using a **first-order Taylor expansion**:

$$
I(x+u,\; y+v) \;\approx\; I(x,y) \;+\; u\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} \;+\; v\,\frac{\partial I}{\partial y}
$$

Substituting into $E(u,v)$:

$$
I(x+u,y+v) - I(x,y) \;\approx\; u\,I_x + v\,I_y
$$

where $I_x = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}$ and $I_y = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}$ are the **image gradients**.

Therefore, the energy function becomes:

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; \sum_{x,y \in W} w(x,y) \, \big[\, u\,I_x + v\,I_y \,\big]^2
$$

## 2.3 The Quadratic Form and the Structure Tensor

Expanding the squared term and collecting the bilinear forms:

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \underbrace{\left( \sum_{x,y} w(x,y) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix} \right)}_{M} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

The $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix $M$ is the **Structure Tensor** (also called the **Second Moment Matrix** or **Harris Matrix**):

$$
M \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \sum_{x,y} w(x,y) \begin{bmatrix} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\ I_x I_y & I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Here, the angle brackets $\langle \cdot \rangle$ denote the **weighted average** over the local window $W$ with weighting function $w$.

## 2.4 Eigenvalue Analysis (Geometric Intuition)

Since $M$ is a real symmetric $2 \times 2$ matrix, it has two **real, non-negative eigenvalues** $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$, with $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The eigenvalues of $M$ are the *principal curvatures* of the auto-correlation surface. They classify the local image structure:

| Case | Eigenvalues | Image Structure |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| Both $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ are **small** | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | **Flat region** (no intensity change in any direction) |
| One **large**, one **small** | $\lambda_1 \gg \lambda_2 \approx 0$ | **Edge** (intensity changes in one direction only) |
| Both are **large** | $\lambda_1 \approx \lambda_2 \gg 0$ | **Corner** (intensity changes in all directions) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it is worth at least 8 marks in any typical 14-mark KTU question on this topic.**

| # | Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Image energy under shift | $E(u,v) = \sum_{W} w(x,y)\,[I(x+u,y+v) - I(x,y)]^2$ | Change in patch intensity under displacement |
| 2 | Taylor approximation | $I(x+u,y+v) \approx I(x,y) + u I_x + v I_y$ | Linearisation for small shifts |
| 3 | **Structure Tensor $M$** | $M = \begin{bmatrix} \sum w I_x^2 & \sum w I_x I_y \\ \sum w I_x I_y & \sum w I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}$ | $2 \times 2$ symmetric matrix of gradient products |
| 4 | Quadratic form | $E(u,v) \approx \begin{bmatrix}u & v\end{bmatrix} M \begin{bmatrix}u \\ v\end{bmatrix}$ | Encodes local intensity change in any direction |
| 5 | Determinant of $M$ | $\det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 = \langle I_x^2 \rangle \langle I_y^2 \rangle - \langle I_x I_y \rangle^2$ | Product of eigenvalues |
| 6 | Trace of $M$ | $\mathrm{trace}(M) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = \langle I_x^2 \rangle + \langle I_y^2 \rangle$ | Sum of eigenvalues |
| 7 | **Harris Response $R$** | $R = \det(M) - k\,[\mathrm{trace}(M)]^2$ | Corner score (scalar per pixel) |
| 8 | Empirical constant | $k \in [0.04,\; 0.06]$, usually **$k = 0.04$** | Balances trace term in $R$ |
| 9 | Interpretation rule | $R > 0$ : **Corner**; $R < 0$ : **Edge**; $\vert R \vert \approx 0$ : **Flat** | Sign of $R$ |
| 10 | Corner criterion | $R > R_{\text{threshold}}$ **and** local maximum | Selects strong, isolated corners |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule Applied:** All vertical bars in formulas (e.g., $\vert R \vert$) are written as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not write `|R|` inside any table cell.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Image Stitching & Panoramas:** Harris corners serve as anchor points for homography estimation in image alignment (Hugin, OpenCV Stitcher pipelines).
- **SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping):** Used in early visual odometry systems to track camera pose from monocular video.
- **Object Recognition:** Pre-deep-learning pipelines (SIFT, SURF) used Harris corners for initial keypoint localization.
- **Medical Imaging:** Registration of CT/MRI scans relies on interest point detectors for aligning pre/post-operative volumes.
- **Robotics:** Visual servoing and robotic grasping use corner tracking for object pose estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From SSD to the Harris Response

### Step 1 — Define the Auto-Correlation Energy

We begin with the **Sum of Squared Differences** of the image patch under displacement $(u, v)$:

$$
E(u,v) = \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,\big[\,I(x+u,\; y+v) - I(x,y)\,\big]^2
$$

> **Logic:** This measures how *different* the shifted patch looks from the original. If $E$ stays small for many $(u,v)$, the patch is repetitive (flat). If it spikes in many directions, we have a *corner*.

### Step 2 — Apply First-Order Taylor Series Expansion

For small $(u,v)$, the shifted intensity is linearised:

$$
I(x+u,\; y+v) \;\approx\; I(x,y) \;+\; \frac{\partial I}{\partial x}\,u \;+\; \frac{\partial I}{\partial y}\,v
$$

> **Logic:** The Taylor series is truncated to first order because higher-order terms become negligible for infinitesimal shifts. This is the *linearisation assumption* at the heart of Harris.

### Step 3 — Compute the Intensity Difference

Subtracting $I(x,y)$ from both sides:

$$
I(x+u,\; y+v) - I(x,y) \;\approx\; u\,I_x + v\,I_y
$$

> **Logic:** The intensity difference is now a *linear combination* of the gradients $I_x, I_y$ with the shift components $u, v$ as coefficients.

### Step 4 — Square the Linear Approximation

$$
\big[\,I(x+u,y+v) - I(x,y)\,\big]^2 \;\approx\; (u\,I_x + v\,I_y)^2
$$

Expanding the square:

$$
(u\,I_x + v\,I_y)^2 \;=\; u^2 I_x^2 \;+\; 2\,u\,v\,I_x I_y \;+\; v^2 I_y^2
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute Back into $E(u,v)$

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,\big[\,u^2 I_x^2 + 2\,u\,v\,I_x I_y + v^2 I_y^2\,\big]
$$

### Step 6 — Distribute the Sum (Linearity of Summation)

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; u^2 \sum w\,I_x^2 \;+\; 2\,u\,v \sum w\,I_x I_y \;+\; v^2 \sum w\,I_y^2
$$

### Step 7 — Collect Terms into a Quadratic Form

Define the weighted sums:

$$
A = \sum w\,I_x^2, \quad B = \sum w\,I_x I_y, \quad C = \sum w\,I_y^2
$$

Then:

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; A\,u^2 + 2\,B\,u\,v + C\,v^2
$$

### Step 8 — Write as a Matrix Quadratic Form

The bilinear form $A\,u^2 + 2\,B\,u\,v + C\,v^2$ is exactly:

$$
E(u,v) \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & C \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Verification (Expanding the matrix product):**
>
> \begin{aligned}
> \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & C \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
> &= \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} A u + B v \\ B u + C v \end{bmatrix} \\
> &= u(Au + Bv) + v(Bu + Cv) \\
> &= A u^2 + B u v + B u v + C v^2 \\
> &= A u^2 + 2B u v + C v^2 \;\;\checkmark
> \end{aligned}

### Step 9 — Identify the Structure Tensor

$$
M \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & C \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \sum w\,I_x^2 & \sum w\,I_x I_y \\ \sum w\,I_x I_y & \sum w\,I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This is the **Structure Tensor** (Harris Matrix). It is symmetric, positive semi-definite, and fully characterises local image geometry.

### Step 10 — Eigenvalue Decomposition

Since $M$ is real symmetric, it can be diagonalised as $M = R\,D\,R^T$ where $D = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2)$ contains the eigenvalues, and $R$ is an orthogonal rotation matrix. The eigenvalues solve:

$$
\det(M - \lambda I) = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; (A - \lambda)(C - \lambda) - B^2 = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic:

$$
\lambda_{1,2} \;=\; \frac{(A+C) \;\pm\; \sqrt{(A-C)^2 + 4B^2}}{2}
$$

> **Logic:** $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ represent the **principal rates of intensity change** along the orthogonal principal directions in the local window.

### Step 11 — Compute Determinant and Trace

$$
\det(M) \;=\; A C - B^2 \;=\; \lambda_1 \lambda_2
$$

$$
\mathrm{trace}(M) \;=\; A + C \;=\; \lambda_1 + \lambda_2
$$

### Step 12 — Construct the Harris Response Function

To avoid the expensive eigenvalue computation, Harris and Stephens proposed a scalar corner score using only $\det(M)$ and $\mathrm{trace}(M)$:

$$
R \;=\; \det(M) \;-\; k\,\big[\mathrm{trace}(M)\big]^2
$$

where $k$ is an empirical sensitivity constant in $[0.04, 0.06]$.

> **Logic (Why this works):**
> - If both $\lambda_i$ are large → $\det(M)$ is large → $R$ is large and **positive** → **Corner**.
> - If one $\lambda$ is large, the other small → $\det(M)$ is small but $\mathrm{trace}^2$ is large → $R$ is **negative** → **Edge**.
> - If both $\lambda_i$ are small → both $\det$ and $\mathrm{trace}^2$ are small → $R$ is approximately **zero** → **Flat**.

### Step 13 — Final Algorithm Pseudocode (Conceptual Outline)

1. Compute image gradients: $I_x = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial x}$, $\;I_y = \dfrac{\partial I}{\partial y}$ (using Sobel/Prewitt kernels).
2. Compute gradient products: $I_x^2,\; I_y^2,\; I_x I_y$.
3. Apply **Gaussian smoothing** ($G_\sigma$) to each product to obtain $A, B, C$.
4. Compute the Harris response $R$ at every pixel:
   $$R(x,y) \;=\; (A\,C - B^2) \;-\; k\,(A + C)^2$$
5. **Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS):** retain only local maxima of $R$ in a $3 \times 3$ or $5 \times 5$ neighbourhood.
6. **Thresholding:** keep only pixels where $R > R_{\text{thresh}}$.
7. The surviving pixels are the **Harris corners**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classification Rule Summary

| Region Type | $\lambda_1$ | $\lambda_2$ | $\det(M)$ | $\mathrm{trace}(M)$ | $R = \det - k\,\mathrm{trace}^2$ | Action |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|---------------------|----------------------------------|--------|
| **Flat** | Small | Small | Small | Small | $\vert R \vert \approx 0$ | Reject |
| **Edge** | Large | Small | Small (≈ 0) | Large | $R < 0$ (large negative) | Reject |
| **Corner** | Large | Large | Large | Large | $R > 0$ (large positive) | **Accept** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Harris Corner Detection Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the full **functional architecture** of the Harris Corner Detection algorithm, mapping the data flow from raw image input to final corner coordinates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT Image I x y] --> B[Stage 1 Gradient Computation]
    B --> B1[Compute Ix using Sobel Kernel]
    B --> B2[Compute Iy using Sobel Kernel]
    B1 --> C[Stage 2 Gradient Product Bank]
    B2 --> C
    C --> C1[Compute Ix squared]
    C --> C2[Compute Iy squared]
    C --> C3[Compute Ix times Iy]
    C1 --> D[Stage 3 Gaussian Smoothing with sigma]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> D1[Form Matrix A = sum w Ix squared]
    D --> D2[Form Matrix B = sum w Ix Iy]
    D --> D3[Form Matrix C = sum w Iy squared]
    D1 --> E[Stage 4 Structure Tensor Assembly M equals AC minus B squared]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Stage 5 Harris Response Map R equals det M minus k trace M squared]
    F --> G[Stage 6 Non Maximum Suppression 3x3 Window]
    G --> H[Stage 7 Thresholding R greater than R threshold]
    H --> I[OUTPUT Set of Harris Corner Coordinates]
```

### 4.1.1 Architectural Stage Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left → right):**
> - **Stage 1:** Extract first-order intensity derivatives along $x$ and $y$ axes via convolution with Sobel/Prewitt kernels.
> - **Stage 2:** Form the three second-order gradient products $I_x^2, I_y^2, I_x I_y$.
> - **Stage 3:** Apply Gaussian smoothing (typically $\sigma = 1$ to $2$ pixels) to reduce noise sensitivity — this yields the matrix components $A, B, C$.
> - **Stage 4:** Assemble the $2 \times 2$ **Structure Tensor** $M = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & C \end{bmatrix}$ at every pixel.
> - **Stage 5:** Compute the scalar **Harris Response** $R = \det(M) - k\,\mathrm{trace}(M)^2$.
> - **Stage 6:** Perform **Non-Maximum Suppression** so that only isolated, strong responses survive.
> - **Stage 7:** Apply a **threshold** $R_{\text{thresh}}$ (e.g., the top 1% of $R$ values) to yield the final corner set.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Eigenvalue Geometry Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1 [Case A: Flat Region]
        A1[lambda 1 small] --> A2[lambda 2 small]
        A2 --> A3[R approx 0]
        A3 --> A4[Classification: FLAT]
    end
    subgraph S2 [Case B: Edge]
        B1[lambda 1 LARGE] --> B2[lambda 2 small]
        B2 --> B3[R strongly negative]
        B3 --> B4[Classification: EDGE]
    end
    subgraph S3 [Case C: Corner]
        C1[lambda 1 LARGE] --> C2[lambda 2 LARGE]
        C2 --> C3[R strongly positive]
        C3 --> C4[Classification: CORNER]
    end
```

### 4.2.1 Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **sign and magnitude** of the Harris response $R$ act as a **single scalar proxy** for the 2D geometry encoded in the eigenvalues of $M$. This elegant reduction is what makes Harris computationally cheap compared to explicit eigenvalue decomposition at every pixel.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a corner point in an image. How does the Harris detector classify a pixel as a corner?** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **corner** is a pixel location where the local image intensity changes significantly in **more than one direction** when the surrounding window is shifted by a small displacement.

The Harris detector classifies a pixel as a corner by computing the **structure tensor** $M = \begin{bmatrix} \langle I_x^2 \rangle & \langle I_x I_y \rangle \\ \langle I_x I_y \rangle & \langle I_y^2 \rangle \end{bmatrix}$ within a local window, then evaluating the response:

$$
R = \det(M) - k\,[\mathrm{trace}(M)]^2
$$

A pixel is declared a **corner** if $R$ is large and positive, and is a local maximum of the response map. (3 marks)

---

### **Q2. What is the structure tensor? Why is it symmetric and positive semi-definite?** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **structure tensor** (second moment matrix) at a pixel is defined as:

$$
M = \begin{bmatrix} \sum w I_x^2 & \sum w I_x I_y \\ \sum w I_x I_y & \sum w I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

It is **symmetric** because $M_{12} = M_{21} = \sum w I_x I_y$. It is **positive semi-definite** because for any vector $\mathbf{v} = [u, v]^T$:

$$
\mathbf{v}^T M\, \mathbf{v} = E(u,v) = \sum w (u I_x + v I_y)^2 \ge 0
$$

Hence all eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2 \ge 0$. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) Part B questions for this module carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice**. The typical split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are two fully independent 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]** — Derive the Harris corner detection algorithm from first principles, starting from the sum-of-squared-differences energy function $E(u,v)$. Show explicitly how the structure tensor emerges.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Starting Energy Function (1 mark):**

$$
E(u,v) = \sum_{x,y} w(x,y)\,\big[\,I(x+u,y+v) - I(x,y)\,\big]^2
$$

**(ii) Taylor Linearisation (2 marks):**

For small shifts,

$$
I(x+u,y+v) \approx I(x,y) + u\,I_x + v\,I_y
$$

Substituting and squaring:

$$
E(u,v) \approx \sum w\,(u^2 I_x^2 + 2 u v I_x I_y + v^2 I_y^2)
$$

**(iii) Quadratic Form & Structure Tensor (2 marks):**

$$
E(u,v) \approx \begin{bmatrix} u & v \end{bmatrix} \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} \sum w I_x^2 & \sum w I_x I_y \\ \sum w I_x I_y & \sum w I_y^2 \end{bmatrix}}_{M} \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(iv) Eigenvalue Interpretation (1 mark):**

If both eigenvalues $\lambda_1, \lambda_2$ of $M$ are large → corner; one large, one small → edge; both small → flat region.

**(v) Harris Response (1 mark):**

$$
R = \det(M) - k\,[\mathrm{trace}(M)]^2 = \lambda_1 \lambda_2 - k(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2)^2
$$

A pixel is a corner iff $R > R_{\text{threshold}}$ and $R$ is a local maximum.

---

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]** — Explain the role of the empirical constant $k$ in the Harris response function. What happens if $k$ is chosen too small or too large? Discuss the algorithm's invariance properties.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Role of $k$ (2 marks):**

The constant $k \in [0.04, 0.06]$ controls the **sensitivity** of corner detection by balancing $\det(M)$ and $\mathrm{trace}^2(M)$. It acts as a **damping factor** on the sum-of-eigenvalues term, preventing edges (one large $\lambda$) from being misclassified as corners.

**(ii) Effect of $k$ (2 marks):**

- If $k$ is **too small** (e.g., $k \to 0$): $R \approx \det(M) = \lambda_1 \lambda_2$, which is small for *both* edges ($\lambda_2 \approx 0$) and flat regions, so detection becomes too permissive — **false positives** on edges.
- If $k$ is **too large** (e.g., $k \to 0.5$): the trace-squared term dominates; genuine corners are suppressed — **false negatives** (missed corners).

**(iii) Invariance Properties (2 marks):**

- **Rotation Invariance:** Because $M$ uses gradient *products* ($I_x^2$, $I_y^2$, $I_x I_y$) and the response is a function of eigenvalues (which are rotation-invariant scalars), Harris is **fully rotation invariant**.
- **Scale Invariance:** Harris is **NOT** scale invariant — a corner at one scale becomes an edge or flat region at another. This is the major motivation for multi-scale detectors like SIFT.
- **Illumination Invariance:** Partially invariant; the detector uses *derivatives*, which are insensitive to additive intensity shifts, but not to multiplicative gain changes.

**(iv) Practical Choice (1 mark):**
